# Organizing a Project from the Shell

Chapter 5 taught you to move through the tree and to make and
copy files. This chapter is about deciding *where* they should
go. A research project is not a heap of files in one folder; it
is a small structure with a place for each kind of thing, so
that six months from now you, or a stranger running your code,
can find the raw data, the scripts, and the results without
asking. The shell is where that structure gets built and
navigated, and building it well is the difference between a
project that reproduces and one that only you can run, and only
this week.

The companion Git book argues at length for *why* a project
should be laid out this way: the replication crisis, the
canonical folder structure that separates inputs from code from
output, and the discipline of a package a stranger can rebuild.
This chapter does not re-argue any of that. It takes the layout
as given and shows how to build it, tour it, and keep it clean
from the terminal: scaffolding the whole tree in one command,
walking it so every file has an obvious home, keeping immutable
raw data apart from regenerated output, and naming things so the
project survives the trip from your Mac to a Linux server. The
tools are the ones Chapter 5 already gave you, `mkdir` and
`ls`, plus one habit: decide the structure once, up front, so
you are never again guessing where a file belongs.

A note on where the output comes from. Every command here runs
in this book's Linux sandbox (Ubuntu 22.04.5, GNU bash 5.1.16,
GNU coreutils 8.32; the `ch05-*.txt` transcript files): a fresh
`/tmp` scratch directory for building a tree from scratch, and
a clean copy of the asset-pricing project for the tour. This
chapter needs no separate macOS capture, because the one place
the platforms diverge, case-insensitive filenames, was captured
on the author's Mac for Chapter 5 and is cross-referenced here
rather than shown twice. The scaffolding tools `copier` and
`cookiecutter` are version-stamped from a real install
(`ch05-scaffold-tools.txt`) but not run, for a reason the last
section explains.

## Scaffold the whole tree in one move: `mkdir -p` with braces

A project needs the same handful of directories every time: a
place for raw data, a place for cleaned data, a place for
scripts, a place for figures, a place for tables. You could
make them one `mkdir` at a time, but the shell can build the
whole tree in a single command, and doing it in one line is
both faster and self-documenting: the command *is* the layout.
Two features from Chapter 5 combine to do it. `mkdir -p`, which
creates every missing parent in a path without complaint, and
**brace expansion**, a shell feature that stamps out a list of
paths from a compact pattern. Making the project root and then
building its subtree in one command:

```bash
$ mkdir asset-pricing
$ cd asset-pricing
$ mkdir -p data/{raw,clean} scripts output/{figures,tables}
$ echo $?
0
$ ls -R
.:
data
output
scripts

./data:
clean
raw

./data/clean:

./data/raw:

./output:
figures
tables

./output/figures:

./output/tables:

./scripts:
```

One `mkdir` built five directories at two levels, and `ls -R`
(recursive, from Chapter 5) walks the result: `data` splits
into `raw` and `clean`, `output` splits into `figures` and
`tables`, and `scripts` stands on its own. The work is entirely
in the braces, and it happens *before* `mkdir` ever runs. The
shell expands each brace group into a list of plain paths and
hands that list to `mkdir`; `mkdir` itself never sees a brace.
You can watch the expansion by handing the same pattern to
`echo`, which just prints what it is given:

```bash
$ echo data/{raw,clean} scripts output/{figures,tables}
data/raw data/clean scripts output/figures output/tables
```

That is the five paths, spelled out. `{raw,clean}` expands to
`data/raw` and `data/clean`, `{figures,tables}` expands the same
way under `output`, and the bare `scripts` passes through
untouched: each comma-separated piece becomes its own word.
Because `mkdir -p` makes every parent along the way, `data` and
`output` are created because their children are. A group can
even nest inside another (`{data/{raw,clean},scripts}` is
legal), though separate groups like these read more clearly.
This one line is the whole scaffold, worth keeping in a
project's README so anyone can recreate the skeleton before
dropping in files.

With the directories in place, the top-level files that live at
the project root can be created as empty placeholders with
`touch` (Chapter 5), to be filled in later:

```bash
$ touch README.md Makefile report.qmd pyproject.toml \
>       .gitignore
$ ls -a
.
..
.gitignore
Makefile
README.md
data
output
pyproject.toml
report.qmd
scripts
```

`ls -a` (all, from Chapter 5) is used deliberately here, because
one of the files just created, `.gitignore`, begins with a dot
and is invisible to plain `ls`. These five are the project's
front matter: a `README.md` that says what the project is and
how to run it, a `Makefile` that builds it (Chapter 12), a
`report.qmd` that renders the paper, a `pyproject.toml` that
pins the Python environment (Chapter 13), and the `.gitignore`
that tells Git what to leave alone. Naming them now, even empty,
stakes out where each concern lives.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
Brace expansion is strict about spaces: there must be none
inside the braces. The shell splits a command into words on
spaces *first*, so a stray space breaks the brace group into
separate words and the expansion silently does not happen.
Watch it fail:

```bash
$ echo data/{raw, clean}
data/{raw, clean}
$ mkdir -p data/{raw, clean}
$ ls
clean}
data
$ ls data
{raw,
```

`echo` gives it away: instead of two paths it printed the
pattern verbatim, braces and all, because the space stopped the
expansion. Then `mkdir` did something worse than fail. It saw
two words, `data/{raw,` and `clean}`, and dutifully made a
directory literally named `data/{raw,` (a `data` folder holding
a subdirectory called `{raw,`) and a second top-level directory
named `clean}`. No error, exit status zero, and a tree that is
quietly wrong. When a brace scaffold produces strange names like
these, a stray space is almost always the cause; write the
pattern with no spaces after the commas.
:::

## A place for everything: a tour of the project

Scaffolding makes the folders; the point of the folders is that
every file then has one obvious home. The asset-pricing project
that runs through this book is laid out exactly this way. Here is
the whole tree at a glance, with each home's job annotated:

```text
asset-pricing/          the project root
|-- data/
|   |-- raw/            immutable inputs (never edited)
|   `-- clean/          derived data (regenerated)
|-- scripts/            the code, in run order (00 to 04)
|-- output/
|   |-- figures/        generated figures
|   `-- tables/         generated tables
|-- Makefile            builds the whole graph (Ch 12)
|-- report.qmd          the Quarto/LaTeX report
|-- README.md           what this is and how to run it
|-- pyproject.toml      Python deps, with uv.lock (Ch 13)
|-- uv.lock             pinned Python versions
|-- renv.lock           R deps, pinned (Ch 13)
`-- .gitignore          what Git leaves untracked
```

That map is the layout, not an aspiration: walking it with the
Chapter 5 commands confirms it is really on disk. Standing at the
project top, plain `ls` gives the shape, and `ls -R` on each
working directory shows the contents:

```bash
$ ls
Makefile
README.md
data
output
pyproject.toml
renv.lock
report.qmd
scripts
uv.lock
$ ls -R data
data:
clean
raw

data/clean:
analysis.parquet
portfolio.csv
portfolio.parquet

data/raw:
factors.csv
firm_panel.csv
$ ls scripts
00_make_data.py
01_clean.py
02_portfolio.py
03_figure.py
04_regression.R
bootstrap_renv.R
check.py
$ ls -R output
output:
figures
tables

output/figures:
cumret.png

output/tables:
ff5_alpha.tex
ff5_alpha.txt
```

The real output matches the annotated map line for line, which
is the point: the structure is legible at a glance
(@fig-ch05-layout). The part worth reading closely is the
three-way split the annotations spell out. `data/raw` holds the
fixed inputs the pipeline starts from (`factors.csv` and
`firm_panel.csv`, the synthetic stand-ins for licensed data);
`data/clean` and `output` hold everything the scripts *derive*
from those inputs, the cleaned data on one side and the finished
figure and table on the other; and `scripts` holds the code in
run order, so the sequence reads off the filenames (`00` through
`03` in Python, `04` in R, plus the helpers). The root files
describe and drive the whole thing, the `Makefile` and
`report.qmd` that build and render it, the `README.md` that
explains it, and the environment locks that pin the Python and R
versions (Chapter 13).

![A research project has one home for each kind of file: fixed raw inputs, the code that reads them, and the results the code regenerates. Raw data flows in, scripts transform it, and every output can be rebuilt, so only the inputs and the code are precious.](../assets/figures/ch05/fig-ch05-layout){#fig-ch05-layout fig-alt="A left-to-right diagram. On the left, a neutral card 'data/raw' labeled immutable inputs. An arrow labeled read leads to a blue card 'scripts' in the center, the numbered code. An arrow labeled write leads to two green cards on the right, 'data/clean' and 'output/ (figures, tables)', labeled regenerated. Below, a neutral band lists the tracked root files: Makefile, report.qmd, README.md, pyproject.toml plus uv.lock, renv.lock, and .gitignore." width="5.6in" fig-pos="H"}

Why this shape and not another is the companion Git book's
argument, in its chapters on the canonical project layout and on
shipping a replication package; the short version is that
separating inputs, code, and output is what lets a stranger
tell, at a glance, what they are allowed to edit and what the
code will regenerate. The terminal's job, and this chapter's, is
to build and move around that structure fluently. Everything
below is about keeping it honest.

## Raw in, generated out

The single most important line in the layout runs between
`data/raw` and everything else: raw data is an **input**, never
an output. Nothing your code does should ever write to, edit, or
delete a file under `data/raw`. The scripts read from it and
write their results elsewhere, to `data/clean` and to `output`,
so that the raw inputs stay exactly as they arrived. This is
what makes the pipeline in the tour a one-directional flow:
`00_make_data.py` produces the raw panel, and from there every
later step reads what exists and writes something new, never
back over its source. One honest wrinkle in this public teaching
project: `00_make_data.py` *generates* the synthetic raw panel,
because real licensed data can never ship inside a repository, so
it stands in for the one-time fetch-or-receive step a real study
performs by hand. The rule holds from that seam onward: once
`data/raw` exists, it is immutable input, and nothing downstream
writes back over it.

That discipline is what makes a project reproducible in the
sense the companion Git book means. If the raw inputs are fixed
and everything else is regenerated from them by code, then the
results are not precious artifacts to be guarded; they are
disposable, rebuildable at any time from inputs plus scripts. A
figure that is wrong can be deleted and remade. A cleaned
dataset that looks off can be traced to the script that wrote it.
The moment you edit a generated file by hand, or edit raw data
in place, that chain breaks and you can no longer say where a
number came from.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
The layout encodes a promise: given the contents of `data/raw`
and `scripts`, everything else can be rebuilt. Chapter 12 makes
that literal, one `make` command regenerates `data/clean` and
all of `output` from the raw inputs and the code, so the results
in the paper are never hand-assembled. Organizing the project
this way is the precondition for that: outputs live in their own
directory precisely so they can be wiped and rebuilt without
touching anything you cannot regenerate. Treat `data/raw` and
`scripts` as the only things worth protecting; treat `data/clean`
and `output` as cache.
:::

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
Two everyday habits quietly destroy reproducibility, and both
are about putting a file in the wrong home. The first is editing
raw data in place: opening a file in `data/raw`, fixing a typo
or dropping a bad row by hand, and saving it. Now the raw input
is something no script produced and no one can reconstruct, and
the "raw" data silently depends on an edit you will not remember.
Do the fix in a script that reads `data/raw` and writes
`data/clean`, so the correction is recorded as code. The second
is letting generated files leak into `scripts` or the project
root: saving a `results.csv` or a `plot.png` next to the code
because it was convenient. Once output is mixed in with source,
you can no longer delete-and-rebuild safely, because you cannot
tell by location what is regenerable. Every file that a command
produces belongs under `data/clean` or `output`, never beside
the code that made it.
:::

## What Git tracks and what `make` rebuilds

Because output is regenerable and raw data is treated like
licensed material that must never be committed, most of the tour
is *not* stored in Git. The `.gitignore` at the project root
draws that line, telling Git to leave `data/raw`, `data/clean`,
and `output` untracked, so the repository holds the code and the
recipe, not the regenerable products or the raw inputs. The
details of what belongs in `.gitignore` and why are the
companion Git book's territory, in its chapter on what must never
be committed; from the shell, the thing to know is which files
are the source of truth (the scripts, the `Makefile`, the
`report.qmd`, the environment locks, the `README.md`) and which
are cache (`data/clean`, `output`) or protected input
(`data/raw`).

That split has a consequence worth understanding, because it
looks like a problem and is actually the design working. Since
those three directories are ignored, a collaborator who clones
the repository gets the tracked files but *none* of the data or
output folders: they are simply not there. In this project that
is deliberate and self-correcting, because the pipeline recreates
each directory the moment it needs to write into it.
`00_make_data.py` creates `data/raw` before writing the raw
panel, `01_clean.py` creates `data/clean`, and the figure and
table steps create `output/figures` and `output/tables`. So a
fresh clone plus one `make` (Chapter 12) rebuilds the whole tree
from nothing, directories included. There is no empty folder to
preserve, because the code lays down its own.

This is where a widely-repeated trick goes wrong, and it is worth
being precise about. Git tracks files, not directories, so a
genuinely empty directory will not survive a commit on its own,
and the usual fix is a placeholder file, conventionally named
`.gitkeep`, that gives the directory something to track. But
`.gitkeep` only works when the directory is *not* ignored.
Dropping a `.gitkeep` into `data/raw` here would do nothing:
`.gitignore` ignores everything beneath that path, the
placeholder included, so Git never sees it. You cannot keep what
you have told Git to ignore. In this project, then, you would not
reach for `.gitkeep` at all; the scripts recreate the ignored
directories. Reserve `.gitkeep` for the other situation, a
directory you *do* want in the repository but that happens to
start empty, such as a tracked `logs/` folder a script writes
into, and never expect it to resurrect an ignored one.

## Names that survive the trip to the server (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

A layout is only as portable as the names in it, and names are
where a project built on a Mac quietly breaks on a Linux server.
Chapter 5 showed the mechanism: the default macOS filesystem
treats `Data.csv` and `data.csv` as the same file, while a Linux
server treats them as two. The layout consequence is concrete. A
script that reads `data/Raw/panel.csv` while the folder on disk
is `data/raw` runs fine on the Mac, where the case does not
matter, and fails with "No such file or directory" the moment
the project is copied to the case-sensitive server it was always
meant to run on.

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
This is Chapter 5's case-sensitivity divergence seen from the
project's point of view, and the defense is a naming convention
rather than a command. Pick one case for every directory and
file in the layout and never deviate, and let lowercase be that
case: `data`, not `Data`; `output`, not `Output`. Avoid spaces
in names too, because a path with a space must be quoted every
time it appears (`"my data/raw"`), and one forgotten quote splits
it into two arguments, the same word-splitting that broke the
brace scaffold earlier; use an underscore or a hyphen instead.
The rule is small and pays off exactly when it is most expensive
to debug, after you have SSH'd the project to a server (Chapter
14) and the pipeline that ran all week on your laptop cannot find
its own folders. The captured macOS side of this collision is in
`transcripts/ch04-case-mac.txt`, from the previous chapter.
:::

## Templating a stable layout: `copier` and `cookiecutter` (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

Once you have built the same scaffold a few times, the `mkdir -p`
one-liner starts to feel like something that should be saved.
Two tools do exactly that: they turn a project layout into a
reusable template you can stamp out a fresh copy from, with the
directories, the placeholder files, the `.gitignore`, and even a
starter `README` already in place. `cookiecutter` is the
long-standing one; `copier` is a newer alternative whose extra
trick is that it can *update* an existing project when the
template later changes. As of 2026-07-02 the current releases are
`cookiecutter` 2.7.1 and `copier` 9.16.0, both installable from
PyPI into a Python environment (`ch05-scaffold-tools.txt`).

This book does not run either tool, and the reason is a matter of
scope rather than difficulty. A template is itself a small
project with its own syntax, placeholder variables, a
configuration file, a directory whose name is a substitution
token, and teaching that syntax would pull the chapter away from
the terminal and into a tool's templating language. What matters
here is the idea and when to reach for it: once your layout has
stabilized across two or three projects, promote it from a
command you retype to a template you instantiate, so every new
study starts from the same clean structure. A template invocation
looks like this, shown only to illustrate the shape, not run:

```text
# illustrative; not executed in this book.
# create a new project from a saved template:
cookiecutter gh:your-org/asset-pricing-template
# or, with copier:
copier copy gh:your-org/asset-pricing-template my-new-study
```

Each command asks a few questions (the project name, the author)
and writes a complete, correctly-named tree, the whole scaffold
of this chapter produced in one step. Until your layout has
settled, though, the `mkdir -p` one-liner is the right tool: it
is portable, it needs nothing installed, and it keeps the
structure in plain sight. Reach for a template when you are
copying the same folders for the third time, not before.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

These build and inspect a project skeleton, so they create
directories and files. Do them in a throwaway `/tmp` directory,
never in a real project. Start each session by moving somewhere
safe to experiment:

```text
# illustrative setup (placeholder path; not a captured run):
mkdir -p /tmp/ch05-play
cd /tmp/ch05-play
```

Everything below happens in there; `/tmp` is cleared on reboot,
so nothing you make will linger.

1. Scaffold the whole tree in one line. Using one `mkdir -p`
   with brace expansion, build `study/` containing `data/raw`,
   `data/clean`, `scripts`, `output/figures`, and
   `output/tables`. Confirm with `ls -R study`. Before you run
   it, predict how many directories the command will create;
   then check with `echo` on the same brace pattern.

2. Trip the space pitfall on purpose. Run `echo
   data/{raw, clean}` with a space after the comma and read what
   prints. Then run the same pattern under `mkdir -p`, list the
   result, and explain in one sentence why you now have
   directories named `clean}` and `{raw,`.

3. Place the front-matter files. With one `touch`, create empty
   `README.md`, `Makefile`, and `.gitignore` at the top of
   `study/`. List the directory two ways, `ls` and `ls -a`, and
   say which file appears only under `ls -a` and why.

4. Test the `.gitkeep` boundary. Create a non-ignored empty
   directory such as `study/logs/`, add `study/logs/.gitkeep`
   with `touch`, and find it with `find study -name .gitkeep`.
   Then explain, in one sentence, why you would *not* put a
   `.gitkeep` under `data/raw`, `data/clean`, or `output` once
   those paths are listed in `.gitignore`.

5. Sort files into their homes. Imagine three files, a raw
   `firm_panel.csv`, a script `01_clean.py`, and a generated
   `cumret.png`. Say which of the five leaf directories each
   belongs in, and which one of the three you would protect from
   ever being overwritten because nothing can regenerate it.
